dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for cardiac arrest/death with a male in Apt # A, --Address Redacted--.
Hello, I'm EMT Alex. I understand you're very concerned about your husband.
Yes, he had a seizure. I called 911 because he stopped breathing afterwards.
I'm now assessing his level of responsiveness. Is he awake or moving on his own?
No, he is not responding at all.
I'll now assess his Glasgow Coma Scale. Can I proceed with this evaluation?
Yes. Please be quick.
His score is 8, with 3 for eye opening, 2 for verbal, and 3 for motor. No obvious reflex or movement response.
His breathing is shallow, so I may assist to support it. Can I begin with the BVM?
Please do.
Assisted respirations started. Gaining adequate chest rise and fall.
He's regaining adequate breathing independently. We'll discontinue assisted ventilation.
Let's confirm his breathing. It appears equal, with normal bilateral breath sounds.
Respiratory rate is 10 per minute with equal chest expansion observed.
Let's assess his circulation. Do you notices any changes or irregularity in his color?
Pulse present at 74 beats per minute. Skin is warm and dry throughout.
Blood pressure is 143 over 82. Skin looks normal in color and condition.
Oxygen saturation is 97%. We'll keep this under continuous observation.
Can you tell me what led to the seizure and when it occurred?
About 30 seconds ago while he was sitting at the table. Nothing unusual happened immediately before it.
Does he have any known medical history related to this?
He has a diagnosis of epilepsy. He had a few seizures earlier this year.
What medications is he taking for this condition?
He's on Keppra, but I'm not sure of the dose or timing.
There is a small laceration at the back of his head. I'll apply a dressing. Is this okay with you?
Pressure dressing applied and bleeding is controlled.
I'll check for head trauma signs, such as pupil response or nosebleeds.
Pupils are 3 mm bilaterally and sluggish. No signs of nosebleed observed.
No bruising around the eyes or signs of a basilar fracture.
I'll assess limb movement and sensation. Is there anything to be aware of?
I've tested all four extremities. No movement or sensation in response to physical stimulation.
Did you see any signs of spinal injury post-seizure?
No signs of spinal injury. He just had the seizure and fell.
I'll check for any pelvic instability or discharge.
His abdomen appears normal in shape and he seems to have no discomfort.
Pelvis is stable with no pain, bleeding, or deformity.
No injuries to the extremities or back noted.
His glucose reading is 90 mg/dL. No immediate changes needed.
Applying cardiac monitoring. Did he have any chest discomfort or concerns?
No, the primary concern was the seizure.
Applying 4-lead EKG now. We're seeing a stable sinus rhythm.
12-lead EKG is in. A left axis deviation is noted, but no acute changes.
Capnography is initiated to monitor end-tidal CO2. No signs of compromise.
I'll attempt to get IV access. May I try an arm or leg?
Please try. His condition is concerning.
We tried three times without success. We'll not attempt it further.
All limbs show no movement or sensation. Postures are maintained.
He's stable now with no signs of suffering from pain or distress.
Given his history and assessment, the seizure protocol is proceeding. Is that okay?
I trust you completely.
[COMPANY_NAME] has treated him previously. They are the best for his condition.
Yes, that's where he was taken before.
We're moving him to a Reeves stretcher to safely transfer into the ambulance.
He's now placed and securely fastened on the stretcher.
Vital signs are stable: BP 126/89, HR 73, and 98% oxygen saturation.
Glucose is at 0 mg/dL. EKG reading remains sinus rhythm.
Capnography shows stable waveform and no signs of airway compromise.
He is now in semi-Fowlers position and secured for transport.
That looks good. Thank you for securing him.
We are contacting [COMPANY_NAME] now and providing them a full report.
The dressing is intact and no further fluid or blood loss detected.
A nurse will be waiting in trauma bed 4. We'll give a full handoff on arrival.
Vital signs are stable during transport. All straps and restraints are in place.
Positioning is secure. We are preparing for arrival.
No medications administered per protocol. Drug waste form is filed.
The patient is being secured into the ambulance. Transport is imminent.
All protocols are in place. We are leaving the scene now.
En route to [COMPANY_NAME]. Calling ahead for trauma bed assignment.
The patient is stable. All lines and supplies are in place.
The patient remains in a semi-Fowlers position. No changes reported.
Re-checking EKG during transport. Sinus rhythm remains stable with no acute changes.
No changes in waveforms. Pre-hospital care is now completed.
We are arriving at the emergency department. Trauma bed 4 is ready.
Patient is secured and ready to unload.
Unload via stretcher into the trauma bay on draw sheet.
The hospital has been advised, and we're finalizing our handoff report.
They advised he was seen at [COMPANY_NAME] twice in the same week.
Drug waste report complete. Nurse has signed care transfer.
All procedures and assessments are complete. All documentation recorded.
Our job here is done. He is ready for hospital care.
